body: Two nearby towns each make something the other wants. Some leaders want to tax goods that cross the border. What should your town do?
choices: Let the towns trade freely with no border tax.
Allow trade, but add a small fee on imports.
Tax imports heavily to protect local sellers.
Ban outside goods and let the town control all trade.
title: Free Markets

body: A new shop wants to sell cheaper shoes than the old shop in town. The old shop asks you to stop the newcomer. What do you do?
choices: Let both shops compete for customers.
Let them compete, but give the old shop a subsidy.
Set one fixed price that both shops must charge.
Have the town run the only shoe store.
title: Free Markets

body: A trader buys goods cheaply in one town and sells them in yours for more. Some call it unfair. What do you do?
choices: Allow it. The trader took a risk and moved goods to where they are wanted.
Allow it, but cap how much profit they can make.
Ban reselling for more than the buying price.
Have the town control who can buy and sell.
title: Free Markets

body: A nearby town sells toys more cheaply. Some shop owners want a tariff, a tax on imports, to protect local makers. What do you do?
choices: Allow the cheaper imports so families pay less.
Add a small tariff on the imports.
Set a high tariff to block them.
Ban goods from other towns.
title: Free Markets & Trade

body: Our town makes bread well, and the next town makes cloth well. Some argue we should produce everything ourselves. What do you do?
choices: Trade bread for their cloth so both towns gain.
Trade a little, but make most things at home.
Stop trading and produce it all locally.
Have officials plan all trade.
title: Free Markets & Trade

body: Leaders want to limit how many toys come in from other towns. What do you do?
choices: Let toys come in freely so prices stay low.
Allow a lot at first, then set a cap.
Set a tight limit on toys coming in.
Ban toys from other towns.
title: Free Markets & Trade

body: Some want a rule that the town can only buy from local sellers. What do you do?
choices: Buy from whoever offers the best deal.
Prefer local sellers a little.
Require buying local most of the time.
Ban buying from outside sellers.
title: Free Markets & Trade

body: Other towns want to buy our grain. Some say keep it all here. What do you do?
choices: Let farmers sell grain to whoever they want.
Allow sales to other towns, but limit them.
Keep most grain from leaving.
Ban selling grain to other towns.
title: Free Markets & Trade

body: Another town sells goods super cheap. Local sellers want them blocked. What do you do?
choices: Let people enjoy the cheaper goods.
Block them only sometimes.
Block the cheap goods to protect sellers.
Ban goods from that town.
title: Free Markets & Trade

body: Two towns each make something the other wants. A leader says stop buying from the other town. What do you do?
choices: Keep trading so both towns get more of what they need.
Trade, but add a small fee on the other town's goods.
Block most goods from the other town.
Let leaders control all trading.
title: Free Markets & Trade

body: Another town taxed our goods. Some want us to tax theirs back hard. What do you do?
choices: Keep our trade open even if they do not.
Add a small matching tax.
Tax their goods back hard.
Ban all trade with that town.
title: Free Markets & Trade

body: Some want only the town government allowed to trade with other places. What do you do?
choices: Let people and shops trade freely themselves.
Allow private trade plus a town trader.
Let mostly the town handle trade.
Make the town the only trader.
title: Free Markets & Trade

body: Your town has money saved up. Some leaders want to spend it all on new programs. Others want to keep taxes low. What is your plan?
choices: Keep taxes low and spend carefully on core needs.
Raise taxes a little to fund several new programs.
Raise taxes a lot to fund many programs.
Have the government take over and direct most spending.
title: Taxes & Spending

body: The town wants to build something new. Leaders argue about how to pay for it. What do you choose?
choices: Fund only what the town can afford without new debt.
Borrow a moderate amount and pay it back slowly.
Borrow heavily now and worry about it later.
Print or seize money to pay for it.
title: Taxes & Spending

body: Leaders propose a special extra tax only on the town's richest families. What do you do?
choices: Keep one fair, low tax rule that applies broadly.
Add a small extra tax on the very richest.
Add a large extra tax on the richest.
Have the town decide each person's share.
title: Taxes & Spending

body: The town wants a new stadium. Should public taxes fund it?
choices: Let private owners finance and build it.
Share the cost between taxes and owners.
Cover most of the cost with taxes.
Fund the whole stadium with taxes.
title: Taxes & Spending

body: Some propose free town buses funded by a new tax. What do you do?
choices: Charge a modest fare so riders fund the service.
Combine modest fares with some tax support.
Make it free by raising taxes.
Have the town run all transportation.
title: Taxes & Spending

body: A big factory asks the town for special tax money to stay. What do you do?
choices: Say no. Treat all businesses the same.
Give a small, short subsidy. A subsidy is money the town gives a business.
Give a large subsidy.
Have the town take over the factory.
title: Taxes & Spending

body: Some want much higher tax rates on people who earn more. What do you do?
choices: Use one low, simple rate for everyone.
Add slightly higher rates at the top.
Set steep rates on higher earners.
Let officials set each person's rate.
title: Taxes & Spending

body: Some want to tax most people to send everyone a check. What do you do?
choices: Keep taxes low so people keep what they earn.
Send a small check paid for by some tax.
Tax a lot to send big checks.
Have the town hand out all income.
title: Taxes & Spending

body: The town wants a big new program paid for by borrowing lots. What do you do?
choices: Spend only what the town can afford now.
Borrow a little for it.
Borrow a lot for the program.
Borrow with no limit.
title: Taxes & Spending

body: The town wants a new project. You can pay from savings or raise taxes. What do you do?
choices: Pay from savings and keep taxes low.
Raise taxes a little to pay for it.
Raise taxes a lot for many new programs.
Take what the town needs from whoever has it.
title: Taxes & Spending

body: Some want a huge tax on a snack they dislike. What do you do?
choices: Let people choose. Share facts, not heavy taxes.
Add a small tax on it.
Put a huge tax on it.
Ban the snack.
title: Taxes & Spending

body: A new program pays so much that some stop working. What do you do?
choices: Help people while keeping work worth it.
Pay a lot with small work rules.
Pay so much that work does not pay.
Pay everyone whether they work or not.
title: Taxes & Spending

body: Some families are unhappy with the only school in town. What policy gives students the best shot?
choices: Let families choose among different schools.
Add charter or specialty schools inside the system.
Just give the existing school much more money.
Force every school to follow one strict government plan.
title: Education

body: A talented student wants a program your town's school does not offer. What do you do?
choices: Let the student use funds at a school that offers it.
Ask the school to add the program if it can.
Tell the student to take what is offered.
Have the government assign all students and programs.
title: Education

body: A group of parents wants to teach their kids at home or in small learning pods. What do you do?
choices: Let families choose home or pod learning if they want.
Allow it, but require heavy paperwork and check-ins.
Discourage it and steer everyone to the main school.
Ban it so all children attend the same school.
title: Education

body: Some families want a voucher, public money they can use at any school, to choose where their kids go. What do you do?
choices: Give families the freedom to choose a school.
Allow vouchers under many restrictions.
Keep students in their assigned school.
Permit only the district school.
title: Education & School Choice

body: Some want every school to follow one identical set of lessons. What do you do?
choices: Let schools and parents choose what works.
Set a few standards, but allow flexibility.
Make most lessons the same across schools.
Force one identical plan on all schools.
title: Education & School Choice

body: Some families want to start a charter school that runs differently. What do you do?
choices: Let charter schools open and compete.
Allow them, but with many rules.
Discourage charters to protect district schools.
Allow only the district school.
title: Education & School Choice

body: Some want every school forced to give the same big test. What do you do?
choices: Let schools and parents judge progress their own way.
Use one light shared test.
Require heavy testing everywhere.
Tie all school choices to one test.
title: Education & School Choice

body: Some want to ban private schools so all kids go to one system. What do you do?
choices: Let private schools exist beside public ones.
Allow them, but with heavy rules.
Discourage private schools.
Ban private schools.
title: Education & School Choice

body: A skilled tutor without a state card wants to teach. What do you do?
choices: Let parents choose tutors they trust.
Allow it, but add some paperwork.
Require a costly state card to tutor.
Allow only state-approved teachers.
title: Education & School Choice

body: Some families want to pick a different school for their kids. What do you do?
choices: Let families choose the school that fits their child.
Allow it, but only from a short approved list.
Make every kid go to the one assigned school.
Let the town decide what every child must learn.
title: Education & School Choice

body: Some families want a savings account to spend on tutors, books, or classes. What do you do?
choices: Let funds follow the child to what they need.
Allow it, but with strict limits.
Keep money only in one system.
Let officials decide every dollar.
title: Education & School Choice

body: Officials want to take over a private college. What do you do?
choices: Let private and public schools both exist.
Take it over, but keep some independence.
Run the college through the town.
Ban private colleges.
title: Education & School Choice

body: Prices in town keep rising fast. People's savings buy less each month. What is your move?
choices: Stop creating extra money so prices can settle.
Create money more slowly while watching prices.
Order shops to freeze their prices.
Print lots of money to hand out to everyone.
title: Money & Inflation

body: The town wants a big festival, and some leaders suggest printing new money to pay for it. What do you do?
choices: Pay for it from real savings, or skip it this year.
Print a little new money just this once.
Print whatever the festival needs.
Print money often to fund fun events.
title: Money

body: Bread is scarce, and some want a price ceiling, a legal limit on the price, to keep it cheap. What do you do?
choices: Let prices adjust so sellers restock quickly.
Set a modest price ceiling.
Impose a strict, very low ceiling.
Have the town ration all bread.
title: Money & Inflation

body: Prices are climbing. Some want to freeze all prices for a year. What do you do?
choices: Let prices adjust and slow the money supply.
Freeze a few prices briefly.
Freeze all prices for a year.
Have officials set every price.
title: Money & Inflation

body: Officials want to mix cheap metal into the coins to make more money. What do you do?
choices: Keep the coins honest and full-value.
Mix in a little cheap metal.
Mix in a lot of cheap metal.
Let the town change the coins anytime.
title: Money & Inflation

body: Politicians want to control money rules to win votes. What do you do?
choices: Keep money rules steady and free of politics.
Let politics nudge the rules a little.
Let politicians set money rules for votes.
Print and spend as leaders wish.
title: Money & Inflation

body: Some want to give everyone free cash by printing new money. What do you do?
choices: Pay only from real money the town has.
Print a little for small payments.
Print lots for big payments.
Print money for everyone forever.
title: Money & Inflation

body: Officials want to fix the money's value by law, even if it is wrong. What do you do?
choices: Let the money's value float freely.
Guide the value a little.
Fix the value by law.
Punish anyone who trades at other rates.
title: Money & Inflation

body: Prices keep going up and money buys less. What do you do?
choices: Keep the money supply steady and stable.
Print a little more money to spend now.
Print a lot of money to pay for programs.
Set the price of everything by law.
title: Money & Inflation

body: Officials want to ban cash and force one town digital money. What do you do?
choices: Let people use cash or apps as they like.
Push digital money, but allow some cash.
Force one digital money on all.
Track and control every payment.
title: Money & Inflation

body: Officials want to force interest rates very low to push cheap loans. What do you do?
choices: Let rates reflect real risk and saving.
Nudge rates down a little.
Force rates far below the real level.
Set all rates from town hall.
title: Money & Inflation

body: A young baker wants to open a shop, but the permits take a year and cost a lot. What do you do?
choices: Cut the red tape so honest businesses can open fast.
Keep the rules, but offer a grant to a few bakers.
Add even more inspections and paperwork.
Have the town open its own bakery instead.
title: Business & Rules

body: To run a food cart, the town requires an expensive license that takes months. What do you do?
choices: Make the food-cart license cheap, quick, and focused on safety.
Keep the license, but waive it for a few carts.
Add more fees and required classes.
Only let the town sell food in public spaces.
title: Business & Rules

body: Ride-share apps now compete with taxis, and the taxi companies want them banned. What do you do?
choices: Let both compete on a level field.
Allow apps under heavy regulations.
Cap how many apps may operate.
Ban the ride-share apps.
title: Business & Regulation

body: Hair braiders must complete a costly, year-long license to work. What do you do?
choices: Drop the requirement so braiders can work freely.
Keep a short, low-cost safety course.
Keep the long, expensive license.
Permit only a few licensed braiders.
title: Business & Regulation

body: A big store wants to open. Local shops want it blocked. What do you do?
choices: Let it open and compete fairly.
Allow it, but with heavy rules.
Block the big store to protect local shops.
Let the town pick which stores may open.
title: Business & Regulation

body: Movers must get a hard, costly license to work. What do you do?
choices: Drop the barrier so movers can start working.
Keep a small, cheap safety check.
Keep the hard, costly license.
Let only a few movers be allowed.
title: Business & Regulation

body: People must get a costly permit to run a business from home. What do you do?
choices: Let people run small home businesses freely.
Require a quick, cheap sign-up.
Keep the costly permit.
Ban businesses run from home.
title: Business & Regulation

body: Opening a small shop needs months of forms and permits. What do you do?
choices: Cut the red tape so shops open easily. Red tape means too many rules.
Keep a short, simple sign-up.
Keep the months of paperwork.
Let officials approve each shop.
title: Business & Regulation

body: A neighbor wants to open a small bakery, but there are lots of forms and rules. What do you do?
choices: Keep the rules simple so it is easy to start.
Add a few extra rules just in case.
Require many permits and inspections.
Let the town run the bakery instead.
title: Business & Regulation

body: After a storm, sellers raise prices on water. Some want a law to forbid it. What do you do?
choices: Let prices rise so more water gets shipped in.
Allow small raises only.
Forbid any price rise.
Have the town hand out all water.
title: Business & Regulation

body: A shop got big by being popular. Some want to break it up. What do you do?
choices: Let it keep winning as long as it competes fairly.
Add extra rules just for being big.
Break up the shop for being big.
Have the town run the big shop.
title: Business & Regulation

body: Some workers in town earn low wages. Leaders want to force a much higher minimum wage. What do you do?
choices: Grow the economy so employers compete for workers.
Raise the minimum wage a small, careful amount.
Force a large minimum wage increase right away.
Have the government set everyone's pay.
title: Jobs & Wages

body: Some leaders want to ban part-time and gig work to 'protect' full-time jobs. What do you do?
choices: Let people choose part-time, gig, or full-time work.
Allow flexible work, but add lots of rules.
Limit gig and part-time work sharply.
Allow only government-approved full-time jobs.
title: Jobs & Wages

body: Some propose a large increase in the minimum wage. What do you do?
choices: Let wages rise as skills and businesses grow.
Raise the minimum modestly.
Force a steep wage increase.
Have officials set every wage.
title: Jobs & Wages

body: Some propose that the town guarantee everyone a government job. What do you do?
choices: Help private businesses grow and hire.
Add some public jobs alongside private ones.
Make most employment government jobs.
Have the town direct all employment.
title: Jobs & Wages

body: Some want every worker forced to join the union. What do you do?
choices: Let workers choose whether to join.
Allow unions, but with some pressure to join.
Require most workers to join.
Make the union control all jobs.
title: Jobs & Wages

body: Some want to force every business to pay for long leave. What do you do?
choices: Let workers and bosses agree on benefits.
Require a small amount of paid leave.
Force a large paid-leave benefit.
Have the town set all benefits.
title: Jobs & Wages

body: Some want a top limit on how much anyone can earn. What do you do?
choices: Let people earn as much as they fairly can.
Allow high pay, but tax the top hard.
Cap how much people can earn.
Have the town set everyone's pay.
title: Jobs & Wages

body: New machines could do some jobs. Some want them banned to save jobs. What do you do?
choices: Allow machines. Workers move to new jobs.
Allow machines, but slow them down.
Ban machines to save old jobs.
Have the town control all machines.
title: Jobs & Wages

body: A teen wants a first job to learn skills. Some want one high pay rule for all jobs. What do you do?
choices: Let workers and bosses agree on pay.
Set a small pay floor. A pay floor is the lowest legal wage.
Set a high pay floor for every job.
Have the town assign everyone a job.
title: Jobs & Wages

body: Some want to force shops to hire by set quotas. What do you do?
choices: Let shops hire the best person for each job.
Set soft hiring targets.
Force strict hiring quotas. A quota is a required number.
Have officials assign all workers.
title: Jobs & Wages

body: Some want a law so businesses can never lay anyone off. What do you do?
choices: Let firms adjust staff as needs change.
Allow layoffs, but add big penalties.
Ban layoffs almost entirely.
Have the town control all hiring and firing.
title: Jobs & Wages

body: A factory is dumping waste into the town river, hurting neighbors downstream. What is the fair fix?
choices: Hold the factory responsible for the harm it causes.
Write detailed rules for exactly how to operate.
Have the government run the factory itself.
Seize the factory and the land around it.
title: Property & Land

body: The town wants to take some families' homes to build a shopping center. What do you do?
choices: Protect homeowners. Build only on land that is sold willingly.
Take the homes, but pay the owners.
Take the homes for the 'greater good'.
Seize whatever land the town wants.
title: Property & Land

body: A factory is polluting the river. What is the fair fix?
choices: Make the factory pay for the harm it causes.
Ask the factory to pollute less voluntarily.
Let the factory pollute without limit.
Have the town take over the factory.
title: Property & Environment

body: A shared lake is being overfished. What is the fair fix?
choices: Assign clear fishing rights so the lake is not drained.
Ask everyone to fish a bit less.
Let anyone take as much as they want.
Have the town seize the entire lake.
title: Property & Environment

body: Rents are high. Some want a law to freeze rents. What do you do?
choices: Let more homes get built to bring rents down.
Freeze rents a little. A freeze stops rents from rising.
Freeze rents hard.
Have the town own all housing.
title: Property & Environment

body: Rules stop people from building new homes on their own land. What do you do?
choices: Let owners build more housing on their land.
Allow building, but with some limits.
Keep strict rules against building.
Have the town decide all building.
title: Property & Environment

body: Some land sits empty. Officials want to seize it. What do you do?
choices: Leave it with its owner to use or sell.
Tax empty land a little.
Take land that sits empty.
Have the town take all empty land.
title: Property & Environment

body: A shared well is running low as everyone takes water. What do you do?
choices: Give clear water rights so people do not waste it.
Ask people to use a bit less.
Let anyone take all they want.
Have the town take the well.
title: Property & Environment

body: A family owns a pond. Some want the town to take it to protect it. What do you do?
choices: Let the owners keep and care for it.
Let them keep it, but add many use rules.
Limit how they can use their own land.
Take the pond for the town.
title: Property & Environment

body: Some want to force landowners to let the public walk through their yards. What do you do?
choices: Respect owners. Ask them to share if they wish.
Force access to a small strip.
Force public access to most land.
Let anyone use any land anytime.
title: Property & Environment

body: Factories pollute. Some want a hard ban, others a market in pollution permits. What do you do?
choices: Use tradable permits so cleanup goes where it is cheapest. Permits let firms buy and sell the right to pollute.
Set one fixed rule for every factory.
Ignore the pollution.
Have the town run every factory.
title: Property & Environment

body: Families want a safe place to save and borrow. How should banking work in your town?
choices: Let trustworthy banks compete and be honest with savers.
Let banks operate, but guarantee every risky loan.
Have the government run the only bank.
Control all money and lending from the top.
title: Banking & Saving

body: Leaders want to cap how much interest banks can charge on loans. What do you do?
choices: Let interest rates reflect real risk and demand.
Cap rates a little for certain loans.
Cap all loan rates well below the market.
Have the town set every interest rate.
title: Banking & Saving

body: A bank that made risky bets is failing. Should the town bail it out?
choices: Let it fail while protecting savers' insured deposits.
Extend a small emergency loan.
Bail the bank out in full.
Have the town run all the banks.
title: Banking & Saving

body: Some propose a new tax on the interest people earn from savings. What do you do?
choices: Keep savings untaxed so families build a cushion.
Tax the interest on savings a little.
Tax the interest on savings heavily.
Have the town hold everyone's savings.
title: Banking & Saving

body: Officials want to force banks to make a set number of risky loans. What do you do?
choices: Let banks lend based on real risk.
Require a few forced loans.
Set big forced loan targets.
Have the town run all lending.
title: Banking & Saving

body: Some want only one town-run bank allowed. What do you do?
choices: Let many banks compete for savers.
Allow a few banks plus a town one.
Allow mostly the town bank.
Make the town bank the only one.
title: Banking & Saving

body: Banks charge fees. Some want a law to cap them low. What do you do?
choices: Let banks compete to offer low fees.
Cap a few fees.
Set strict low fee caps.
Have the town set all bank fees.
title: Banking & Saving

body: Officials want to limit how much money people can take out of the bank. What do you do?
choices: Let people use their own money freely.
Limit big withdrawals only.
Cap how much people can take out.
Freeze people's accounts.
title: Banking & Saving

body: You want people to save money for the future. What do you do?
choices: Let banks compete and keep saving simple.
Promise to cover every risk with town money.
Tax the interest people earn from saving.
Have the town hold everyone's savings.
title: Banking & Saving

body: Some want a fee on money people keep in savings to make them spend. What do you do?
choices: Let people save freely without a penalty.
Add a small fee on big savings.
Charge a fee on most savings.
Take a share of idle savings.
title: Banking & Saving

body: Some want a rule that banks may only lend inside our town. What do you do?
choices: Let banks lend where it makes the most sense.
Prefer local loans a little.
Require almost all loans stay local.
Have the town direct all loans.
title: Banking & Saving

body: A newspaper prints an article criticizing town leaders. Some want it shut down. What do you do?
choices: Protect the right to speak, even when it is critical.
Allow it, but require that articles be approved first.
Ban articles that make leaders look bad.
Take over the press and control the news.
title: Free Speech

body: A controversial speaker wants to talk in the town square. Some residents want them banned. What do you do?
choices: Allow the speech. Let people listen, argue, or walk away.
Allow it, but require approval of the topic first.
Ban speakers most people disagree with.
Allow only town-approved speakers.
title: Free Speech

body: Some residents want a book removed from the public library. What do you do?
choices: Keep the book and let people choose what to read.
Keep it, but move it out of sight.
Remove books many people dislike.
Stock only officially approved books.
title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties

body: Some want residents to get a town permit before holding a march. What do you do?
choices: Let people assemble and speak without a permit.
Require a brief, free notice for large crowds.
Require a permit officials may deny.
Allow only marches the town approves.
title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties

body: Some want newspapers to get a town license to print. What do you do?
choices: Let anyone publish without a license.
Require a simple sign-up to publish.
Require a license officials can deny.
Allow only town-approved papers.
title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties

body: Some want everyone forced to say a town message they may not believe. What do you do?
choices: Let people speak, or stay silent, freely.
Ask for the message, but allow opt-outs.
Require most people to say it.
Punish people who will not say it.
title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties

body: Some want to ban people from criticizing town leaders. What do you do?
choices: Let people criticize leaders freely.
Allow it, but punish rude words.
Ban harsh criticism of leaders.
Punish anyone who speaks against leaders.
title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties

body: Some want town officials to approve posts before people share them online. What do you do?
choices: Let people post without asking first.
Allow posts, but review some first.
Require approval before posting.
Allow only approved messages online.
title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties

body: People say things you disagree with in the town square. What do you do?
choices: Let people speak, even if you disagree.
Allow speech, but require a permit first.
Ban topics the town finds upsetting.
Only let the town's message be shared.
title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties

body: Some want to ban shops from advertising their prices and deals. What do you do?
choices: Let shops tell people about their goods.
Allow ads, but limit them a lot.
Ban most ads.
Let only town-approved ads run.
title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties

body: Some want the town to pick which charities may ask for donations. What do you do?
choices: Let any honest charity ask for support.
Allow it, but with heavy sign-up rules.
Let only approved charities collect.
Ban charities the town dislikes.
title: Free Speech & Civil Liberties

body: Care in town is getting expensive. People disagree on how to make it affordable. What do you choose?
choices: Let providers compete and let prices be clear.
Keep the system, but add government subsidies.
Tighten rules and limit which treatments are allowed.
Have the government run all care and set all prices.
title: Health Care

body: Skilled nurses could handle more basic care, but strict rules say only doctors may. What do you do?
choices: Let trained nurses do more of what they are qualified for.
Loosen the rules a little for some tasks.
Keep strict limits on who can treat patients.
Have the government assign all care tasks.
title: Health Care

body: Medicine is cheaper in a neighboring town. Should residents be allowed to buy it there?
choices: Allow people to buy safe, cheaper medicine.
Permit it under heavy restrictions.
Block the cheaper medicine.
Have the town be the only seller of medicine.
title: Health Care

body: A rule caps how many new clinics may open, and a nurse wants to start one. What do you do?
choices: Let new clinics open and compete.
Permit a few new clinics under rules.
Keep the cap on new clinics.
Have the town operate all clinics.
title: Health Care

body: Medicine costs a lot. Some want the town to set low prices by law. What do you do?
choices: Let more sellers compete to lower prices.
Cap a few medicine prices. A cap is a legal limit.
Set strict low prices on medicine.
Have the town make all medicine.
title: Health Care

body: Some want to force everyone to buy one health plan. What do you do?
choices: Let people pick the plan that fits them.
Require a basic plan with some choice.
Force everyone into the same plan.
Have the town provide the only plan.
title: Health Care

body: Some want one government office to be the only place for care. What do you do?
choices: Let many clinics and doctors compete.
Allow private care plus a big public office.
Make most care go through one office.
Make the government the only provider.
title: Health Care

body: A charity clinic offers cheap care. The board wants to shut it. What do you do?
choices: Let the clinic keep helping patients.
Allow it, but add heavy rules.
Limit the clinic to protect hospitals.
Shut the charity clinic.
title: Health Care

body: People want good, affordable care. What do you do?
choices: Let doctors and patients choose, and let clinics compete.
Add rules that set some prices.
Have the town run most clinics.
Force every doctor to follow town orders.
title: Health Care

body: Some want to ban private clinics so all care is public. What do you do?
choices: Let private and public care exist together.
Allow private care, but with heavy rules.
Discourage private clinics.
Ban private clinics.
title: Health Care

body: Some want to force doctors to accept only government-set fees. What do you do?
choices: Let doctors and patients agree on fees.
Set fees, but allow some exceptions.
Force one fee schedule on all doctors.
Have the town employ all doctors.
title: Health Care
